title: I Feared the People
reading:
Saul said it plainly: I feared the people and obeyed their voice. 1 Samuel 15:24. It is one of the most honest confessions in the Old Testament — and one of the most costly. God had given Saul a direct command. Saul disobeyed it, not out of ignorance, but out of fear of what people would think. The crowd mattered more than the command.

The result was devastating. Samuel's word to Saul was that the kingdom had been torn from him. A moment of people pleasing cost him everything.

Aaron's story in Exodus 32 follows the same pattern. Moses had gone up the mountain to meet with God. In his absence, the people grew anxious and demanded something visible to worship. Aaron — the high priest — folded. He collected their gold. He fashioned the calf. He gave the crowd what they wanted, even though what they wanted was idolatry.

Both men knew better. Both men had seen God work. And both men, in a pressure moment, chose the approval of the crowd over obedience to God.

People pleasing is not a small habit. In both cases it led directly to sin — because you cannot serve two masters, and the crowd makes a terrible one.
reflection: When have you obeyed the voice of the crowd instead of the voice of God? What were the consequences — visible or invisible?
abide: Lord, I do not want to be ruled by the fear of people. Give me the courage to obey You, even when it costs me approval.
